county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records well preserved from formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Wasco County in 1889. Named for General William T. Sherman. Moro has been the county seat since formation. One of Oregon's least populated counties. Dryland wheat farming dominates. The Columbia River forms the northern boundary; the Deschutes River the western boundary. The John Day Dam and associated flooding displaced some communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sherman County is one of Oregon's smallest and most rural counties, making records manageable but sparse. The county clerk in Moro has deed and marriage records from 1889. For pre-1889 records, check Wasco County (parent). BLM GLO homestead patents document the dryland wheat farming settlers who arrived in the 1870s–1890s. The Sherman County Historical Museum in Moro has family files and wheat farming community records. FamilySearch has limited microfilm for Sherman County. The Columbia River communities along the northern border were affected by the construction of the John Day Dam in the 1960s — some towns were relocated or flooded. The Oregon Trunk Railway and Great Southern Railroad brought settlers to the area. Church records are limited due to the sparse rural population.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; in-office access only

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Wheat farming homesteads and ranching land.
Federal land patents for dryland wheat farming homesteads in the Columbia Plateau.
Marriage records from county formation. Very small volume.
Federal census 1890–1950 (1890 destroyed). Very small population.

source_profiles.source_name: Sherman County Journal and Moro Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Moro area newspapers covering wheat farming and rural community news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Limited digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire. Records intact from county formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Clatsop, Yamhill, and Polk counties in 1853. Named for the Tillamook people. Tillamook has been the county seat since formation. Famous for the dairy industry (Tillamook Cheese). The Tillamook Burn — a series of catastrophic forest fires (1933, 1939, 1945, 1951) — destroyed vast timber stands. Swiss and other European dairy farming families settled here. The Wilson River provides the main access route from Portland.
county_metadata.research_tips: Tillamook County's genealogical research centers on the dairy farming industry that defined the community. Swiss, Danish, and other Northern European families settled here in the late 1800s to establish dairy farms. The Tillamook County Pioneer Museum has an outstanding collection of family files, pioneer records, and dairy industry history — one of Oregon's best small museums. The county clerk has deed and marriage records from 1853. The Tillamook Burn forest fires (1933–1951) displaced timber workers and loggers — some employment records are at the Oregon State Forestry Department archives. FamilySearch has microfilmed Tillamook County records. The Tillamook County Creamery Association (Tillamook Cheese) records document generations of dairy farming families. Coastal fishing and cannery records supplement land-based records. Check Clatsop, Yamhill, and Polk counties (parents) for pre-1853 records of the area.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; limited online access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes dairy farm land transactions and timber holdings.
Marriage records from county formation. Includes Swiss, Danish, and other immigrant farming families.
Federal census 1860–1950 (1890 destroyed). Shows dairy farming communities and Northern European immigrant families.

source_profiles.source_name: Tillamook County Church Records — Swiss and Scandinavian Congregations
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Church records from Swiss Reformed, Lutheran, and other congregations serving the dairy farming community. Baptisms, marriages, and burials.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial — some records at OHS, others still held by individual congregations.

source_profiles.source_name: Tillamook Headlight-Herald and Tillamook County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tillamook newspapers covering dairy industry, Tillamook Burn fires, and coastal community news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records well preserved from formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Wasco County in 1862. Named for the Umatilla people. Pendleton has been the county seat since 1868 (Marshall, now abandoned, was the first seat). The Confederated Tribes of the Umatilla Indian Reservation (Umatilla, Cayuse, and Walla Walla peoples) are headquartered near Pendleton. The Pendleton Round-Up (rodeo, since 1910) is nationally famous. Wheat farming and ranching county. The Oregon Trail passed through the county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Umatilla County research involves both the Euro-American wheat farming and ranching communities and the Confederated Tribes of the Umatilla Indian Reservation (CTUIR). The county clerk in Pendleton has deed and marriage records from 1862. The Tamástslikt Cultural Institute (CTUIR's museum) preserves tribal perspectives on the Oregon Trail and reservation era. BIA records at NARA Seattle for the Umatilla Agency are extensive — covering Umatilla, Cayuse, and Walla Walla peoples with treaty records (1855), census rolls, allotment records, and agency correspondence. The Pendleton Round-Up archives document rodeo families and western heritage. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Oregon Trail's passage through the county (especially the Blue Mountains crossing) is documented in emigrant diaries at the Oregon Historical Society. Chinese workers were present in railroad construction and mining — check NARA Seattle for Chinese Exclusion case files. The Umatilla Army Depot records are at NARA.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; limited online access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes wheat farming homesteads and Umatilla Reservation allotment transfers.

source_profiles.source_name: Confederated Tribes of the Umatilla — BIA and Treaty Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA records for the Umatilla, Cayuse, and Walla Walla peoples. Treaty records (1855), census rolls, allotment files, and Umatilla Agency correspondence.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent — NARA Seattle holds comprehensive Umatilla Agency records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation.
Federal census 1870–1950 (1890 destroyed). Umatilla Reservation residents have separate BIA census rolls.
The East Oregonian (founded 1875) and other Pendleton newspapers covering wheat farming, Round-Up, and reservation news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records well preserved from formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Baker County in 1864. Named in honor of the Union cause during the Civil War. La Grande has been the county seat since 1874 (earlier seat at La Grande Ronde Valley). The Grande Ronde Valley was a major Oregon Trail stopover. Eastern Oregon University is in La Grande. Timber and agriculture (cattle, wheat) are primary industries. The Wallowa Mountains form the eastern boundary.
county_metadata.research_tips: Union County sits in the Grande Ronde Valley, a key Oregon Trail stopover, and emigrant diaries frequently describe the valley — these are at the Oregon Historical Society and at university special collections. The county clerk in La Grande has deed and marriage records from 1864. The Union County Museum in Union has family files and local history. Eastern Oregon University in La Grande has regional history collections. BLM GLO homestead patents document farming and ranching settlers. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Oregon Trail emigrant experience through the Blue Mountains into the Grande Ronde Valley is documented in journals, and the Oregon Trail Interpretive Park at Blue Mountain Crossing preserves trail ruts. Logging in the Blue Mountains generated employment records scattered across private collections. Chinese miners worked in the gold fields near Union — check NARA Seattle for Chinese Exclusion case files. Check Baker County (parent) for pre-1864 records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; limited online access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Grande Ronde Valley farming and ranching land.
Federal land patents for Grande Ronde Valley farming and ranching homesteads.
Marriage records from county formation.
Federal census 1870–1950 (1890 destroyed). Shows Oregon Trail settlement pattern in Grande Ronde Valley.

source_profiles.source_name: La Grande Observer and Union County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: La Grande and Union newspapers covering farming, ranching, and Blue Mountains timber industry.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records preserved from formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Union County in 1887. Named for the Wallowa River (from Nez Perce 'winding water'). Enterprise has been the county seat since 1888 (Joseph was briefly the first seat). The Wallowa Valley was the homeland of the Nez Perce led by Chief Joseph — the 1877 Nez Perce War and forced removal is central to the county's history. Ranching, logging, and tourism in the Wallowa Mountains ('Oregon's Switzerland').
county_metadata.research_tips: Wallowa County research is inseparable from the history of the Nez Perce people and Chief Joseph. The Wallowa Valley was the homeland of the Wal-lam-wat-kain (Wallowa) band of the Nez Perce, and Chief Joseph's resistance and the 1877 Nez Perce War are documented in military records at NARA, BIA records at NARA Seattle, and the Nez Perce National Historical Park archives. The county clerk in Enterprise has deed and marriage records from 1887. The Wallowa County Museum in Joseph has family files, ranching records, and Nez Perce history materials. Ranching families who settled after the Nez Perce removal are documented in BLM GLO homestead patents. FamilySearch has limited microfilm for Wallowa County. The Wallowa Mountains timber industry is documented in Forest Service records at NARA. Check Union County (parent) for pre-1887 records. The town of Joseph is named for Chief Joseph and has an active bronze foundry arts community today.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; in-office access only

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Ranching homesteads in the Wallowa Valley — land taken from the Nez Perce.

source_profiles.source_name: Nez Perce / Chief Joseph Band — BIA and Military Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA Seattle / Nez Perce National Historical Park
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA records for the Nez Perce people including the Wallowa band. Treaty records (1855, 1863), 1877 Nez Perce War military records, and removal documentation. Nez Perce National Historical Park has interpretive materials.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA holds both BIA and military records. Nez Perce Tribe in Lapwai, Idaho holds tribal records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Ranching community families.
Federal census 1890–1950 (1890 destroyed). Small population throughout.

source_profiles.source_name: Wallowa County Chieftain and Enterprise Newspapers

source_profiles.survival_notes: Limited digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire. Records well preserved from formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Clackamas and Marion counties in 1854. Named for the Wasco people (Chinookan). Originally encompassed all of eastern Oregon — the largest county in U.S. history. The Dalles has been the county seat since formation. The Dalles was a critical Oregon Trail waypoint where emigrants transferred from overland wagons to Columbia River boats. Fort Dalles (1850–1867) was the principal military installation in eastern Oregon.
county_metadata.research_tips: Wasco County was once the largest county in U.S. history, encompassing all of eastern Oregon, so records from the 1850s–1880s may include areas later assigned to Baker, Grant, Umatilla, Crook, Gilliam, Sherman, Hood River, Jefferson, Deschutes, Wheeler, and other counties. The county clerk in The Dalles has deed and marriage records from 1854. The Columbia Gorge Discovery Center in The Dalles has Oregon Trail emigrant documentation and local history collections. Fort Dalles military records (1850–1867) at NARA document soldiers and the transition from emigrant waypoint to military post. The Dalles was where Oregon Trail emigrants faced the final choice: raft down the Columbia River or take the Barlow Road around Mount Hood. Donation Land Claims for Wasco County are at BLM GLO. FamilySearch has extensive microfilmed records. The Dalles' cherry orcharding industry brought seasonal workers. Chinese residents are documented in census records and Chinese Exclusion Act files.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; online index available

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from formation. Early records cover vast eastern Oregon territory before subdivision into daughter counties.
Fort Dalles post records, muster rolls, and personnel files. The fort was the principal military installation in eastern Oregon during the Indian Wars era.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA holds complete post records. Fort Dalles Museum has supplementary materials.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Donation Land Claim files for Wasco County settlers. The Dalles area claims are among the easternmost DLCs in Oregon.
Marriage records from county formation. Early records cover vast territory.
Federal census 1860–1950 (1890 destroyed). Early censuses cover vast eastern Oregon territory before county subdivisions.

source_profiles.source_name: The Dalles Chronicle and Wasco County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: The Dalles newspapers covering Oregon Trail terminus, military affairs, and Columbia River commerce.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records well preserved from Provisional Government era.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the four original districts created by the Provisional Government in 1843 (originally named Tuality, renamed Washington in 1849). Named for George Washington. Hillsboro has been the county seat since 1850. Now Oregon's second most populous county and part of the Portland metropolitan area. Originally agricultural (Tualatin Valley), now home to the Silicon Forest (Intel, Nike headquarters in adjacent area). The Tualatin people (Atfalati Kalapuya) originally inhabited the area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Washington County is one of Oregon's original Provisional Government districts (1843, as Tuality District), so records extend to the earliest period of American governance in Oregon. The county clerk in Hillsboro has deed and marriage records from the 1840s. Donation Land Claims for Tualatin Valley settlers are extensive at BLM GLO Records. The Washington County Museum has pioneer family files, photographs, and agricultural history. The county's rapid suburbanization since the 1960s means many older farmland records are relevant for tracking land ownership transitions. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm of Washington County records from the territorial era. The Provisional Government records at Oregon State Archives include Tuality District proceedings. Helvetia was a Swiss farming community whose records may include church records in German/French. The Hillsboro Argus newspaper (1873) provides continuous local coverage. For the Tualatin people, check Siletz and Grand Ronde reservation records at NARA Seattle.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County recording division with deed records from Provisional Government era; online access available

source_profiles.source_name: Washington County Records Division — Land Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from Provisional Government era (as Tuality District). Among Oregon's oldest land records.
Donation Land Claim files for Tualatin Valley settlers — among the most numerous in Oregon.
Marriage records from Provisional Government era.
Federal census 1850–1950 (1890 destroyed). 1850 territorial census includes Washington County (as Tuality).

source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — older records at Oregon State Archives.

source_profiles.source_name: Hillsboro Argus and Washington County Newspapers

source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records preserved from formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Crook, Gilliam, and Grant counties in 1899. Named for Henry H. Wheeler, a mail carrier and Indian fighter. Fossil has been the county seat since formation. Oregon's least populated county (fewer than 1,500 residents). Ranching and wheat farming. The John Day Fossil Beds National Monument is partly within the county. The Painted Hills are a significant geological feature.
county_metadata.research_tips: Wheeler County is Oregon's least populated county, making records extremely manageable but sparse. The county clerk in Fossil has deed and marriage records from 1899. For pre-1899 records, check Crook, Gilliam, and Grant counties (parents). BLM GLO homestead patents document ranching and farming settlers. The Wheeler County museum in Fossil has a small but focused local history collection. FamilySearch has very limited microfilm for Wheeler County. The John Day River communities were connected by stock drive routes and rural roads rather than railroads — transportation infrastructure records are limited. The county's extremely low population means that every census enumeration is easily searchable and individual families can be traced through the complete sequence. Church records are rare due to the sparse settlement pattern.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; in-office access only

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Ranching and wheat farming homesteads.
Federal land patents for ranching and farming homesteads in the John Day River area.
Marriage records from county formation. Very small volume — Oregon's least populated county.
Federal census from 1900 (first after county formation). Extremely small population — entire county easily searchable.

source_profiles.source_name: Fossil Journal and Wheeler County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Fossil area newspapers — small-town weeklies covering ranching and rural community news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Very limited digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Provisional Government era records survive well.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the four original districts created by the Provisional Government in 1843. Named for the Yamhill River and the Yamhill (Yamel) band of the Kalapuya people. McMinnville has been the county seat since 1857 (earlier seat at Lafayette). Willamette Valley agricultural county, now famous for wine production. Lafayette was the original county seat. The Yamhill-Carlton area is Oregon's premier wine region. Part of the Grand Ronde Reservation is in Yamhill County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Yamhill County is one of Oregon's original Provisional Government districts (1843), giving it some of the oldest records in the state. The county clerk in McMinnville has deed and marriage records from the 1840s. The county seat moved from Lafayette to McMinnville in 1857, and records were transferred — but researchers should check both towns for contextual records. Donation Land Claims for Yamhill County settlers are extensive at BLM GLO Records. The Yamhill County Historical Society in Lafayette has pioneer family files, photographs, and early settlement history. The Confederated Tribes of Grand Ronde Reservation extends into western Yamhill County — BIA records at NARA Seattle cover this community. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm of Yamhill County records from the territorial era. Linfield University (founded 1858 as McMinnville College) in McMinnville has university archives with local connections. The Yamhill Valley wine industry is a modern development; historically this was grain, fruit, and dairy farming country.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from Provisional Government era; limited online access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from Provisional Government era — among Oregon's oldest land records. Includes Donation Land Claim transfers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — excellent preservation from the earliest period.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Donation Land Claim files for Yamhill County settlers — among the most numerous in Oregon's Willamette Valley.
Marriage records from Provisional Government era — among Oregon's earliest civil marriage records.
Federal census 1850–1950 (1890 destroyed). 1850 territorial census includes Yamhill County.

source_profiles.repository: Yamhill County Circuit Court / Oregon State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate files from the Provisional Government era. Pioneer settlers' estates with detailed inventories.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — older records at Oregon State Archives.

source_profiles.source_name: McMinnville News-Register and Yamhill County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: McMinnville and Lafayette newspapers covering agriculture, pioneer settlement, and Willamette Valley community news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.
